name: local-forward
context: Expose a remote service on a local port with ssh -L for databases, dashboards, and private APIs
info: Local forwarding is the most common SSH tunnel pattern: the client listens on a local port and forwards traffic over the SSH session to a destination host and port as seen from the remote side. Agents use it to reach private Postgres instances, internal admin panels, or service endpoints through a bastion without exposing the remote service publicly.
triggers: forward a remote service to my laptop
body:
# Local Forwarding

A local forward binds a port on the client and sends connections across SSH to a destination host and port from the remote side.

```bash
ssh -L 5433:127.0.0.1:5432 ops@db-gateway.example.com -N
```

After the tunnel is up, connect your local client to `127.0.0.1:5433` and SSH carries the traffic to `127.0.0.1:5432` from the gateway host.

Common uses:

- reach a private [[postgres]] instance
- inspect an internal HTTP admin interface
- call a service available only inside the remote network

If the listening port should stay private on the client, omit `SSH_BIND_ADDRESS` or set it to `127.0.0.1`.

sections.title: Tunnel a private database
sections.body: A classic pattern is `ssh -L 5433:127.0.0.1:5432 bastion -N`, then pointing the local database client at `127.0.0.1:5433`. The destination host and port are resolved from the remote side, so `127.0.0.1` usually means the SSH host itself unless the host can reach another internal target.

sections.title: Choose the listening bind address
sections.body: Without an explicit bind address, SSH follows local defaults. Setting `SSH_BIND_ADDRESS=127.0.0.1` keeps the tunnel local-only. Binding to `0.0.0.0` or `*` exposes the local listening port to other machines on the client network and should be done deliberately.
